title: List Tables in a SQLite Database
hook: Before querying a database file, see what tables are actually inside it.
problem: You need a quick inventory of tables in a SQLite database.
starting_state: A local app.db file exists and you need to inspect it from the terminal.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command asks SQLite to list table names.
when_to_use: Use when you inherit a SQLite file or need quick orientation during debugging.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to inspect views or detailed schema; follow up with .schema.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: A compact list of table names in the database.

shorts.title: List SQLite tables fast.
shorts.voiceover: When you get an app.db file, start with dot tables so you know what data exists before querying.
shorts.caption: Start SQLite triage with .tables.

linkedin.question: What is your first command when someone hands you a SQLite file?

youtube.description: Use sqlite3 .tables to list tables in a local SQLite database.

title: Show One SQLite Table Schema
hook: A failed query is often just a wrong assumption about column names.
problem: You need to inspect the schema for one SQLite table.
starting_state: A SQLite database contains a users table and you need its columns and indexes.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints schema statements for the selected table.
when_to_use: Use before writing ad hoc SQL against a table you do not know well.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a migration history; it shows current schema, not how it got there.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: CREATE TABLE and related index statements for the users table.

shorts.title: Check one table schema.
shorts.voiceover: Before guessing column names, ask SQLite for the table schema and indexes directly.
shorts.caption: Use .schema before ad hoc SQL.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect table schema before writing incident queries?
linkedin.cta: Let SQLite show the table shape first.

youtube.description: Use sqlite3 .schema to inspect one table in a SQLite database.

title: Check SQLite Database Integrity
hook: When a SQLite-backed app behaves strangely, first rule out file corruption.
problem: You need a read-only integrity check for a SQLite database.
starting_state: A local SQLite database file may be involved in an app failure.
what_changed: Nothing changes. SQLite runs an integrity check and reports the result.
when_to_use: Use after crashes, disk issues, interrupted copies, or unexplained database errors.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat a passing result as a full application diagnosis; it only checks database structure.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: The word ok when the database passes the integrity check.

shorts.title: Check SQLite integrity.
shorts.voiceover: If a SQLite app gets weird after a crash, run PRAGMA integrity_check before chasing application code.
shorts.caption: Integrity check first.

linkedin.question: Do you run integrity checks after SQLite-backed apps crash?

youtube.description: Run PRAGMA integrity_check from sqlite3 to verify a SQLite database file.

title: List SQLite User Tables Only
hook: System metadata tables can distract from the app tables you care about.
problem: You need to list normal tables from sqlite_master.
command: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name;"
starting_state: A SQLite database contains application tables and migration metadata.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command queries sqlite_master for table names.
when_to_use: Use when .tables output is too compact or you need a script-friendly list.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to list indexes or views without changing the type filter.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: One table name per line, sorted by name.

commands: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table' ORDER BY name;"

shorts.title: Script-friendly table list.
shorts.voiceover: Query sqlite_master when you want table names one per line instead of compact dot tables output.
shorts.caption: sqlite_master gives scriptable metadata.

linkedin.question: Do you prefer .tables or sqlite_master when scripting SQLite checks?
linkedin.cta: Use sqlite_master when output shape matters.

youtube.description: Query sqlite_master to list SQLite user tables in a script-friendly format.

title: Count Rows in Key SQLite Tables
hook: A quick row count can reveal empty imports, runaway events, or missing data.
problem: You need row counts for several important SQLite tables in one command.
command: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT 'users', count(*) FROM users UNION ALL SELECT 'orders', count(*) FROM orders UNION ALL SELECT 'events', count(*) FROM events;"
starting_state: A SQLite database contains users, orders, and events tables.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads row counts from selected tables.
when_to_use: Use during data sanity checks, imports, restores, or incident triage.
when_not_to_use: Do not run huge counts blindly on very large production databases without understanding cost.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Pipe-separated table names and row counts.

commands: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT 'users', count(*) FROM users UNION ALL SELECT 'orders', count(*) FROM orders UNION ALL SELECT 'events', count(*) FROM events;"

shorts.title: Count key tables fast.
shorts.voiceover: Union a few count queries when you need quick data sanity checks across important SQLite tables.
shorts.caption: Fast sanity check: table row counts.

linkedin.question: Do you check row counts after restoring a SQLite backup?
linkedin.cta: Use small count queries for a first sanity pass.

youtube.description: Count rows from multiple SQLite tables in one sqlite3 command.

title: Show Indexes on a SQLite Table
hook: Slow lookups often start with missing or misunderstood indexes.
problem: You need to inspect indexes attached to one SQLite table.
starting_state: A SQLite orders table may be involved in slow lookups.
what_changed: Nothing changes. SQLite prints index metadata for the table.
when_to_use: Use before changing queries or adding indexes during performance triage.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it alone to prove an index is used; follow up with EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Index rows showing index name, uniqueness, origin, and partial-index flag.

shorts.title: List SQLite table indexes.
shorts.voiceover: Use PRAGMA index_list when you need to see which indexes a table actually has.
shorts.caption: Inspect indexes before changing queries.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect existing indexes before adding a new one?
linkedin.cta: Check table indexes before performance changes.

youtube.description: Use PRAGMA index_list to inspect indexes on a SQLite table.

title: Show Recent SQLite Events
hook: For small apps, the quickest timeline may be inside the SQLite file.
problem: You need the most recent event rows from a SQLite events table.
command: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT created_at, event_type FROM events ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5;"
starting_state: A SQLite database has an events table with timestamps and event types.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads recent rows from the events table.
when_to_use: Use when reconstructing a simple timeline from local app data.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a replacement for durable logs if the table is sampled or pruned.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Recent timestamps and event types, newest first.

commands: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT created_at, event_type FROM events ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5;"

shorts.title: Read recent SQLite events.
shorts.voiceover: When a tiny app stores events in SQLite, order by timestamp and limit the output for a quick timeline.
shorts.caption: SQLite can hold the incident timeline.

linkedin.question: Do your small apps keep enough event data for local timeline checks?
linkedin.cta: Use recent rows as one incident clue.

youtube.description: Query recent event rows from a SQLite events table.

title: Count SQLite Events by Type
hook: A noisy event type stands out faster when you group it.
problem: You need to summarize event counts by event_type in SQLite.
command: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT event_type, count(*) FROM events GROUP BY event_type ORDER BY count(*) DESC;"
starting_state: A SQLite events table contains multiple event types.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command groups event rows by type and counts them.
when_to_use: Use when checking event mix, noisy actions, or whether one failure type dominates.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it for long-range analytics without filtering by time when the table is large.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Event types with counts, ordered from most common to least common.

commands: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT created_at, event_type FROM events ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5;"
sqlite3 app.db "SELECT event_type, count(*) FROM events GROUP BY event_type ORDER BY count(*) DESC;"

shorts.title: Group SQLite events.
shorts.voiceover: Group events by type to see whether one action or failure is dominating the database.
shorts.caption: Count events by type.

linkedin.question: When event volume changes, do you group by type before reading rows?

youtube.description: Group and count SQLite event rows by event type.

title: Find Duplicate Emails in SQLite
hook: Duplicate account data is easier to spot with one grouped query.
problem: You need to find duplicate email values in a SQLite users table.
command: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT email, count(*) FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING count(*) > 1;"
starting_state: A users table may contain duplicate email rows after an import or bug.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command groups users by email and prints repeated values.
when_to_use: Use after imports, failed uniqueness migrations, or account merge bugs.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete duplicates from this output alone; inspect IDs and references first.
undo: No undo needed because this command is read-only.
expected_output: Email values with counts greater than one.

commands: sqlite3 app.db "SELECT email, count(*) FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING count(*) > 1;"

shorts.title: Find duplicate emails.
shorts.voiceover: Group by email and use HAVING to print only values that appear more than once.
shorts.caption: Find duplicates before fixing data.

linkedin.question: Do you check duplicates after CSV imports into SQLite?
linkedin.cta: Find duplicate keys before changing rows.

youtube.description: Use GROUP BY and HAVING to find duplicate emails in SQLite.

title: Back Up a SQLite Database File
hook: Copying a live SQLite file blindly can produce a bad backup.
problem: You need to create a SQLite backup through sqlite3 instead of a raw file copy.
starting_state: A local app.db file exists and a backup directory is available.
what_changed: SQLite writes a backup copy to backup/app.db.
when_to_use: Use before risky data work, migrations, or manual inspection on a SQLite-backed app.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as your only production backup plan; automate and verify backups separately.
undo: Remove the backup file with rm backup/app.db if it was created only for a temporary check.
expected_output: The sqlite3 backup command is quiet on success; verify with ls afterward.

shorts.title: Back up SQLite safely.
shorts.voiceover: Use SQLite's backup command before manual data work, then verify the backup file exists.
shorts.caption: Use .backup instead of a blind copy.

linkedin.question: Do you use SQLite .backup before manual database fixes?
linkedin.cta: Create and verify a backup before risky data work.

youtube.description: Use sqlite3 .backup to create a local SQLite database backup file.
